title: Spirit Airlines Collapses as Warren Buffett Amasses Record War Chest

blocks.content: Spirit Airlines ceased operations after a last-ditch financing deal collapsed, marking the first major airline bankruptcy since the pandemic era, while Berkshire Hathaway revealed a record $397 billion cash stockpile in a stark reminder that the market's most legendary investor is sitting on the sidelines.

Spirit Airlines said early Saturday it had no choice but to wind down operations immediately after a White House-backed rescue deal fell through, ending 56 years of service and leaving thousands of passengers stranded mid-journey. The collapse of the low-cost carrier underscores the brutal pressures facing budget airlines as persistent inflation and weakening consumer demand make it impossible to service mounting debt loads, raising questions about which airline could be next on the chopping block.

Meanwhile, Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway posted a doubling of operating earnings in Greg Abel's first quarter as CEO, but the headline number that caught Wall Street's attention was the $397 billion cash pile sitting idle, signaling that neither Buffett nor Abel sees attractive opportunities in current markets, a historically cautious signal that has preceded major market dislocations.
• S&P 500: Ticked lower as airline stocks crumbled and investors digested the Fed's hawkish pivot
• FTSE 100: Stood flat as European markets weighed mixed signals from the U.S.-Iran tensions
• Nasdaq: Fell 0.8% as technology names slipped amid the broader risk-off sentiment
• SPIRIT AIRLINES: The budget carrier shut down immediately after a rescue deal backed by the White House collapsed, stranding passengers and ending 56 years of flight operations.

• BERKSHIRE HATHAWAY: Warren Buffett revealed a record $397 billion cash pile as operating earnings doubled under new CEO Greg Abel, signaling the Oracle of Omaha sees no compelling opportunities in current markets.
• FEDERAL RESERVE: Jerome Powell and the FOMC updated their interest rate outlook with a historic level of dissent, marking what analysts are calling a dubious turning point for markets.
• YEN INTERVENTION: Japanese authorities deployed $34.5 billion to prop up the yen, triggering its sharpest rally in months and putting currency traders on high alert for further action.
Traders will focus on Iran peace talks developments as oil markets assess whether crude futures have further to fall on hopes of de-escalation.
